coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical immunosensor for ultra-sensitive detection of attomolar prostate specific antigen with sulfur-doped graphene quantum dot@gold nanostar as the probe
coredata.dc:creator: Tran, Hai Linh
Darmanto, Win
Doong, Ruey-An
coredata.dc:description: A novel sulfur-doped graphene quantum dot (S-GQD) decorated 3-dimensional gold nanostar (AuNS/S-GQD) nanocomposite has been developed as the actively electrochemical immunosensing material for ultra-sensitive and selective detection of attomolar prostate specific antigen (PSA) in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and human serum. The S-GQDs were hydrothermally prepared at 180 °C for 10 h, and then AuNS/S-GQDs were fabricated by reducing Au precursor to AuNS in the presence of S-GQDs at room temperature. The electronic microscopic results indicate that 2 – 8 nm S-GQDs are homogenously deposited onto the 35 – 60 nm AuNS surface. The AuNS provides an excellent electric conductivity for electrochemical performance, while S-GQD serves as a sensing probe to bind prostate specific antibody (Ab) via amide bonding. After deposition of AuNS/S-GQDs onto screen-printed electrode (Es), the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor provides a stable, ultra-sensitive, and highly selective platform for PSA detection. The wide detection ranges of 10 fg mL−1 – 50 ng mL−1 and 100 fg mL−1 – 35 ng mL−1 with low limits of detection (LOD) of 0.29 (9.7 aM) and 26 fg mL−1 in PBS and neat human serum, respectively, are obtained. Moreover, the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor can effectively detect PSA in human serum with recovery of 97 – 107%. The high selectivity toward PSA detection over the other 8 interferences and low cytotoxic effect of Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor are also observed. These excellent results demonstrate the potential of using AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposite as an electrochemically biosensing probe to effectively detect PSA and other cancer markers in human serum for clinically early diagnosis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor for PSA detection in phosphate buffer saline and human serum.

The TEM images of (a) S-GQDs (inset: histogram of S-GQDs), (b) AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites (scale: 50 nm with the histogram of AuNS/S-GQDs), (c) AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites (scale = 20 nm), (e) HRTEM image with lattice spacing of S-GQDs and AuNS/GQDs, (d) DLS of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs, (e) AFM image of S-GQDs, (g) and (h) the height distribution range of S-GQDs and Au/S-GQDs, and (k) AFM image of AuNS/S-GQDs.

The XPS analysis of (a) full survey scan of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites, deconvolution of (b) C 1s, and (c) S 2 peaks of S-GQDs, and (d) Au 4f, (e) C 1s, and (f) S 2p peaks of AuNS/S-GQDs, (g) FT-IR spectra, (h) XRD patterns, and (i) Raman spectra of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQD nanomaterials.

(a) LSV curves of Es||AuNS/S-GQDs at various scan rates of 5 – 200 mV s−1, (b) the stepwise electrochemical performance of Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab electrode, and the Nyquist plots of (c) S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites onto the Es, and (d) the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor in 0.1 M PBS containing 0.1 M KCl and 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4− at pH 6.5.

(a) LSV current intensity of Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab at various PSA concentrations of 0 – 50 ng mL−1, (b) calibration curve of PSA detection from 10 fg mL−1 to 50 ng mL−1 in PBS, the linear range of LSV current intensity at (c) low (10 fg mL−1 to 0.15 ng mL−1), (d) high (0.15 – 50 ng mL−1) PSA concentrations in PBS, and the calibration curves at (e) low (0.1 pg mL−1 to 0.2 ng mL−1) and (f) high (0.2 – 35 ng mL−1) PSA concentrations in neat human serum by the Es||AuNS/S-GQD based immunosensor.

(a) The effect of pH on the stability of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor before and after the addition of 20 ng mL−1 PSA, (b) the stability of Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor during the detection of 50 ng mL−1 PSA at different storage periods, (c) the selectivity of AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor in the presence of different interferences and PSA, and (d) cytotoxicity assays of TRAMP-C1 cells incubated with various concentrations of AuNS/S-GQDs at 0 – 1000 µg mL−1.
Comparison of the analytical performance of the Es||AuNS/S-GQDs immunosensor with reported sensors for PSA detection.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical immunosensor for ultra-sensitive detection of attomolar prostate specific antigen with sulfur-doped graphene quantum dot@gold nanostar as the probe

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel sulfur-doped graphene quantum dot (S-GQD) decorated 3-dimensional gold nanostar (AuNS/S-GQD) nanocomposite has been developed as the actively electrochemical immunosensing material for ultra-sensitive and selective detection of attomolar prostate specific antigen (PSA) in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and human serum. The S-GQDs were hydrothermally prepared at 180 °C for 10 h, and then AuNS/S-GQDs were fabricated by reducing Au precursor to AuNS in the presence of S-GQDs at room temperature. The electronic microscopic results indicate that 2 – 8 nm S-GQDs are homogenously deposited onto the 35 – 60 nm AuNS surface. The AuNS provides an excellent electric conductivity for electrochemical performance, while S-GQD serves as a sensing probe to bind prostate specific antibody (Ab) via amide bonding. After deposition of AuNS/S-GQDs onto screen-printed electrode (Es), the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor provides a stable, ultra-sensitive, and highly selective platform for PSA detection. The wide detection ranges of 10 fg mL−1 – 50 ng mL−1 and 100 fg mL−1 – 35 ng mL−1 with low limits of detection (LOD) of 0.29 (9.7 aM) and 26 fg mL−1 in PBS and neat human serum, respectively, are obtained. Moreover, the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor can effectively detect PSA in human serum with recovery of 97 – 107%. The high selectivity toward PSA detection over the other 8 interferences and low cytotoxic effect of Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor are also observed. These excellent results demonstrate the potential of using AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposite as an electrochemically biosensing probe to effectively detect PSA and other cancer markers in human serum for clinically early diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid detection of low-level cancer markers for the early diagnosis and therapy of tumors is currently of a great scientific significance. Prostate-specific antigen (PSA), an effective biomarker for prostate cancer, is a naturally occurring single-chain glycol-protein enzyme that is encoded by the KLK3 gene in humans with a molecular weight of approximately 30 kDa [1,2]. Prostate cancer is credited as one of the main causes of cancer death in males, and the PSA concentration under 4 ng mL−1 (0.13 nM) is considered healthy in males [3]. Therefore, the detection of PSA at low concentration is urgently important for early diagnosis of prostate-related diseases. Although several techniques like mass spectrometric immunoassay [4] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [5] are currently used to diagnose prostate cancer, these methods are costly, tedious, complicated, and labor-intensive [6]. Therefore, the searching of a rapid and accurate sensing technique to effectively identify the low concentration of PSA in human serum is urgently needed.

To fulfill the requirement of rapid and accurate detection of low level of PSA, nano-biosensors with different detection techniques such as colorimetric [7], fluorescent [8], photoelectrochemical [9–11], and electrochemical methods [12–16] have been developed to sensitively and selectively detect PSA. Among those methods used, the electrochemical biosensor is one of the most efficient devices to determine PSA due to its high sensitivity, wide detection range, short response time, and ease of use. Several metal nanoparticles (MNPs) and carbonaceous nanomaterials such as gold (Au) [17], silver (Ag) [18], platinum (Pt) [19], graphene family [20], and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [21,22] have been used as sensing materials for the optical and electrochemical detection of different analytes. Among MNPs, Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been increasingly utilized in electrochemically sensing application because of its strong electrical conductivity, fast electron transfer kinetics, and biocompatibility [23–26]. However, AuNPs are susceptible to aggregate easily, leading to a significant reduction in their sensing capacity [27,28] 3-Dimensional (3-D) Au structures such as Au nanostar (AuNS) and Au nano-dendrite have, therefore, been developed for optical detection of analytes by minimize the particle aggregation [29,30]. Furthermore, the 3-D Au structures have a unique physicochemical property with more nano-scale branches, high conductivity and fast electron transfer kinetics, which is beneficial for electrochemical sensing purpose [23–25]. However, the use the 3-D Au based structures as the biosensing material for electrochemical detection of cancer markers has yet received attention.

The combination of Au nano-materials with graphene family to preserve the sensing activity has been reported [31,32]. GQDs, the developed zero-dimensional (0-D) member of the carbon family, are promising electrode materials because of their excellent optical, electrical, and chemical properties [33–35]. Additionally, the doping of GQDs with heteroatoms such as N, S, and B can drastically enhance the electron density on the surface framework, resulting in the improvement of sensing sensitivity and selectivity toward a wide variety of analytes detection [36–38]. Anh and Doong have used S-GQDs to directly reduce Au3+ ions into AuNPs, and the Au@S-GQDs served as an effective sensing probe for the determination of 4-nitrophenol in environmental media [31]. It is noteworthy that sulfur atoms onto GQD surface have a strong affinity toward AuNPs, and the S-GQDs are capable of enhancing the sensitivity of immunosensor by linking the antibody (Ab) through carboxylic-amine bonding. Therefore, the combination of 3D AuNS with S-GQDs (AuNS/S-GQDs) governs a well-defined 3D architecture with unique characteristics including high biocompatibility, enhanced stability, fast electron transfer, and less toxicity. However, the anchoring of S-GQDs onto 3D AuNS to form an electrochemical immunosensor for efficient and effective detection of PSA has not been reported yet.

In this study, the AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites with enhanced electrochemical activity were fabricated for ultra-highly sensitive and selective detection of attomolar PSA in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and human serum samples. As shown in Scheme 1 , the AuNS/S-GQDs, prepared by hydrothermal and reduction methods, were deposited onto the surface of screen-printed electrode (Es) to form the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor for PSA detection by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). To achieve the excellent sensitivity toward attomolar PSA detection, the immunosensor was applied in a potential window of −0.3 – 0.8 V at 100 mV s−1 in 0.1 M PBS at pH 6.5. The novel Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor exhibited the superior sensitivity toward PSA detection. A wide linear range of 6 orders of magnitude from 10 fg mL−1 to 50 ng mL−1 with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.29 fg mL−1 (9.7 aM) in PBS is obtained. In addition, the sensitivity of PSA detection over 8 different interferences was examined. The excellent long-term stability and high biocompatibility of AuNS/S-GQDs active materials can open an alternative gateway to functionalize the AuNS/S-GQDs based nanocomposites for ultra-sensitive and selective detection of a wide variety of cancer markers in biological samples.

Aurochloric acid (HAuCl4⋅3H2O, 99.99%), mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA, C4H6O4S, 97.0%), α-D-glucose (C6H12O6, 96.0%), prostate-specific antigen from human semen (PSA, ≥ 95.0%), and anti-PSA antibody produced in rabbit were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Potassium ferricyanide(III) (K3[Fe(CN)6], 99.8%) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.0%) were acquired from J. T. Baker. Potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]⋅3H2O, 99%) was bought from Riedel-de Haën. 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT, C18H16BrN5S, 98%) was obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Human serum AB (0.1 μM) was obtained from Gemini Bioproducts. All other chemicals and solvents were used as purchased. Deionized water (DI water, 18.2 MΩ cm) was used for the preparation of solutions.

S-GQDs were initially hydrothermally prepared at 180 °C for 10 h according to our previously reported work. For the synthesis of 3D AuNS/S-GQDs, 7.7 mL of HEPES buffer (180 mM) and 2 mL of 0.15 mg mL−1 S-GQDs solution were mixed vigorously. After that, 0.3 mL of 25 mM HAuCl4⋅3H2O solution was immediately added to the solution and then vortexed for 30 s to synthesize 3D AuNS/S-GQDs. After vortexing, the solution color turned into a dark blue color, indicating the successful reduction of Au3+ to Au°. The solution was then kept to sit undisturbedly for 30 min in the dark to completely form the AuNS/S-GQDs composites. Afterward, the excess reagents and unattached S-GQDs were washed and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites were obtained after 24 h of freeze-drying.

The AuNS/S-GQD powder was mixed with PVDF and carbon black at the AuNS/S-GQD:carbon black:PVDF weight ratio of 7:0.5:0.4 to prepare the working electrode. The solution was then mixed with 200 μL of NMP, ultra-sonicated for 3 h, and then followed by drop-casting of 5 μL of 35 mg mL−1 AuNS/S-GQDs electrode material onto the electrode. The electrode was dried at 37 °C for 30 min for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab based immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The immunosensor was prepared by adding 9 μL of 3.5 mg mL−1 poly-L-lysine (PL) onto the working electrode of Es||AuNS/S-GQDs, dried naturally in air and then washed thoroughly with DI water to eliminate the un-loaded chemicals on the electrode surface. Afterward, the Es||AuNS/S-GQDs were incubated with an anti-PSA antibody by casting 9 μL of 10 μg mL−1 antibody solution onto the working electrode. PL was introduced to immobilize the antibody by linking the amine groups in antibodies with the –COOH groups on S-GQDs. After washing with PBS, the nonspecific binding sites of sensing materials was blocked by using 1 wt% bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution. After 1 h of reaction, various concentrations of PSA in the range of 10 fg mL−1 – 50 ng mL−1 were added to the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab electrode. After 30 min of incubation, the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab/PSA electrode was rinsed with 0.1 M PBS at pH 6.5 to eliminate the un-loaded PSA molecules and then detected by LSV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PSA in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites over PSA detection in biomedical application was investigated in human serum samples. Human serum was diluted 10 × with 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.4 to prepare the stock solutions by spiking various concentrations of PSA into the diluted human serum to get the final PSA concentration of 10 fg mL−1 – 50 ng mL−1. The PSA concentration and recovery in human serum were recorded and calculated by the change in LSV current signal. For the human serum sample experiments, all the procedures were approved by the ethics committee and the institute review board (IRB) of National Tsing Hua University (IRB No. 10802HM004). Additionally, all the analyses were carried out at least triplicates for quality control (QC) of the electrochemical immunosensor system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical determination of PSA by immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical performance of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV), LSV and the charge transfer capability of immunosensor was performed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The LSV was measured in the potential window of −0.3 – 0.8 V at 100 mV s−1. The impedance of active materials was measured in a frequency range of 0.1 – 10,000 Hz. The 0.1 M PBS at pH 6.5 containing 0.1 M potassium chloride (KCl) and 0.01 M of the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- complex was used as the electrolyte.

The selectivity of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor was examined in the presence of various proteins and biomolecules including α-fetoprotein (AFP), Tau protein, BSA, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), hemoglobin (Hb), L-cysteine, glutathione, and choline as the interferences. The Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor was incubated with the different interferences at high concentration to ensure the excellent selectivity of the immunosensor. The AFP, Tau protein, BSA, and CEA are incubated at 100 ng mL−1, while Hb, L-cysteine, glutathione, and choline was used at 10 mg mL−1. Comparatively, a low PSA concentration of 30 ng mL−1 was used for the experiments.

The biocompatibility and toxicity of AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites toward cell viability was evaluated by the MTT assay method. The viability of TRAMP-C1 cells were assessed in the presence of various concentrations (0, 100, 200, 350, 500, 750, and 1000 μg mL−1) of AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites and MTT reagent. The TRAMP-C1 at 105 cells mL−1 was introduced and keep at 37 °C for 24 h under the atmosphere containing 5% CO2. Thereafter, the TRAMP-C1 cells were washed with 0.1 M UPSB medium at pH 7.4 and then incubated for 48 h. The cell viability of TRAMP-C1 cells was determined using a micro-plate reader.

The morphology of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites were obtained using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with JEOL JEM-ARM-200F equipped with a cold field emission gun and a JEOL JEM-2010 high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). Additionally, the hydrodynamic size of AuNS/S-GQDs based nanomaterial was measured by a Zetasizer-Nano ZS dynamic light scattering (DLS). Furthermore, Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were obtained by an Agilent 5500 AFM in tapping mode to analyze the change in topography as well as to calculate the thickness of S-GQDs in AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites. The absorption properties of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs were determined by using a UV-visible spectrophotometer (HITACHI U-4010). Raman spectroscope (UniRAM-2200) was used to determine the ratio of D and G bands of the S-GQDs, and the AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposite. Moreover, X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was employed to decipher the chemical compositions as well as the elemental species of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCA Ulvac-PHI 1600). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded to identity to the functional groups of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs in a wavenumber range of 450 – 4,000 cm−1 using a Horiba FT-720 spectrophotometer. In addition, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were characterized from 15 to 90° 2θ by a Bruker D8 advance mode X-ray diffractometer with radiation source of Cu Kα at 1.5406 Å. All the electrochemical experiments including LSV and EIS were carried out using Autolab PGSTAT 302N (Metrohm Autolab B.V.) electrochemical system.

In this study, the shape and the size distribution of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQD nanomaterials was examined first. The TEM image indicates that S-GQDs are successfully synthesized and the diameter is in the range of 2 – 8 nm with a mean particle size of 5 ± 1 nm ( Fig. 1
a). After the combination with 3-D AuNS, the AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites illustrated in Fig. 1b shows the star-shaped configuration of AuNS with a measured diameter of 35 – 60 nm and a mean particle size of 50 ± 5 nm (inset of Fig. 1
b). From the HR-TEM image of the AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites ( Fig. 1
c), it can be seen that the S-GQDs are successfully decorated on the surface as well as the edge of AuNS. The characteristic lattice spacing of 0.21 nm for graphene can be distinguished from that of AuNS at 0.235 nm (Fig. S1, Supplementary data). The fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs also corroborate that AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites exists as a single crystal with a spacing equivalent to that of graphene (Fig. S2, Supplementary data).

In addition, the hydrodynamic diameters of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs were determined by DLS. As shown in Fig. 1
e, the S-GQDs show a sharp peak with a mean particle size 3.5 ± 2.5 nm, while AuNS/S-GQDs display a broad peak at 48.5 ± 3.6 nm. This result is similar to the particle size distribution of TEM images of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs ( Fig. 1
a,b). Moreover, the energy dispersive spectra (EDS) clearly indicates the existence of C, O, S, and Au elements in AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites (Fig. S3, Supplementary data). The weight ratios of C, O, S, and Au are 13.9, 6.4, 6.4, and 73.3 wt%, respectively. AFM was further used to examine the topographic surface and the thickness of S-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1f, the AFM image displays the well-dispersed S-GQDs and the height distribution range is 1.4 – 3 nm, which clearly indicates that the S-GQDs is 4 – 8 layers of graphene in thickness ( Fig. 1
g). The AFM image of AuNS/S-GQD composites once again demonstrates the successful decoration of S-GQDs on the surface of AuNS with a rough thickness of 27 – 32 nm ( Fig. 1
h–k), which is an indication of the successful synthesis of AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites. As the S-GQDs can be linked with AuNS via Au-thiol bonding, S-GQDs are uniformly dispersed on the surface of AuNS, and play a critical role in homogeneously loading of antibody onto AuNS/S-GQDs as they can facilitate the attachment of anti-PSA antibody onto S-GQDs.

The XPS spectra was performed to confirm the chemical state of elements in S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites. The survey scan spectra of S-GQDs ( Fig. 2
a) display the typical O 1s, C 1s, S 1s, and S 2p peaks located at 531, 284, 228, and 163 eV, respectively. The presence of S 1s and S 2p indicates the successful modification of GQDs with S element [31,34]. After the formation of AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites, additional peaks contributed from Au 4d at 354 and 336 eV, and Au 4f at 87 eV are clearly observed. The XPS peaks of O 1s, C 1s, and S 2p in S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites were further deconvoluted. For S-GQDs, the C 1s exhibits four peaks at 283.7, 285.2, 287.7, and 289.0 eV, which can be assigned as the C=C/C-C, C-O/C-H/C-S, C=O/C-OH, and COOH functional groups [39], respectively, ( Fig. 2
b). The deconvolution of O 1s spectrum of S-GQDs illustrates peaks at 531 (COOH), 532.0 (C=O), and 533 eV (O-C-O/C-OH), clearly corroborating the presence of carboxylic groups on the structure of GQDs (Fig. S4a, Supplementary data) [40]. Moreover, four peaks at 162.3 (C-S), 163.2 (C-S-C), 163.9 (S-H), and 164.5 eV (C-S-O) are observed in the S 2p spectrum of S-GQDs, which proves again the successful doping of S element into GQDs ( Fig. 2
c) [31,36].

The XPS of C 1s peak in the AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposite shows similar patterns to that of S-GQDs after deconvolution. However, the C=C peak slightly shifts to the more oxidized state of 284.2 eV, which is an indication of the successful linkage of AuNS with S-GQDs by electron flow from S-GQDs to AuNS ( Fig. 2 e). The species of the deconvoluted O 1s peak is found to be similar to that of S-GQDs, but the intensity of O-C-O/C-OH and C=O peaks diminishes obviously (Fig. S4b, Supplementary data). The S 2p spectrum shows peaks at 162.5 (Au-S), 164.3 (S-H), 167.6 (C-SO2), and 186.2 eV (S-O) ( Fig. 2
f). The peak at 162.5 eV confirms the formation of Au-S linkage. Moreover, the deconvoluted Au 4f peaks at 87 and 83.2 eV are the typical Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2 of Au0, respectively ( Fig. 2 d) [41].

FTIR was performed to decipher the presence of functional groups in S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites. As can be seen in Fig. 2
g, the broad peak of S-GQDs at 3455 cm−1 is the characteristic O-H group. Moreover, the presence of C=O (1078 cm−1) and C-O (1045 cm−1) means that the S-GQDs are naturally hydrophilic. Besides, the peaks located at 2940 (methyl symmetrical C-H group), 1625 (C=C), and 1387 cm−1 (aldehyde C-H group) belong to the graphitic backbone of the S-GQDs [42]. Peaks at 2338 (S-H), 1134 (C=S), 988 (S=S), and 619 (cm−1) (C-S) clearly indicate that S atoms are successfully doped into GQDs framework [31,34]. The FTIR spectrum of AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites is similar to that of S-GQDs. However, an extra peak at 721 cm−1, which is contributed from Au-S thiol linkage, is also observed. Moreover, the sulfur-containing peaks including C=S, S=S, and C-S show a slight red-shift in wavenumber, i.e., from 1134 to 1192 cm−1, from 988 to 1192 cm−1, and from 619 to 685 cm−1, respectively, signifying the linkage of S-GQDs onto the AuNS surface. The crystallinity of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs are also identified. As illustrated in Fig. 2
h, the XRD analysis of S-GQDs displays a wide peak located at 2θ = 24.85°, which is the characteristic (002) plane of graphene [31]. After the formation of AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites, the graphene peak shifts to 25.25° 2θ. Additionally, peaks at 38.30°, 44.40°, 64.78°, 77.72°, and 81.88° 2θ corroborates the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes of metallic Au, respectively [43, 44]. Moreover, the UV absorption spectra of S-GQDs and AuNS/S-GQDs confirm the existence of AuNPs in the AuNS/S-GQDs (Fig. S5, Supplementary data). In addition, Raman spectroscopy was used to characterize the shift in locally elemental symmetry after the formation of AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites. The Raman spectrum of S-GQDs shows the G and D bands at 1556 and 1366 cm−1, respectively, with the ID/IG ratio of 0.91, which indicates that the S-GQDs possess a crystalline graphitized nature graphene [34, 45]. The D and G bands of AuNS/S-GQDs shift to 1370 and 1568 cm−1, respectively, and the peak intensity of G-band increases obviously with the decreased ID/IG ratio of 0.88 ( Fig. 2
i), clearly elaborating that the interaction between AuNS and S-GQDs increases the in-plane vibration of sp2 of carbon in S-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical property of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
After the successful fabrication and characterization, the AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites were immobilized onto Es to fabricate the immunosensor, and the electro-chemical properties including CV, LSV and EIS were examined. The electrochemically active surface area of the screen-printed platinum electrode (Es), pristine SGQD and AuNS nanomaterials, and AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites was first estimated from the CV curve of each material in the presence of K3[Fe(CN6)] as the oxidation-reduction probe by using the Randles-Ševčik equation:
(1)
I
p a
=
( 2.69 ×
10 5
)
m
3 2
D
1 2
A
v
1 2
C
where Ipa is the peak current, A is the effective surface area, C is the electrolyte concentration of K3[Fe(CN6)], m is the number of electrons transferred (n=1), D is the diffusion coefficient of K3[Fe(CN6)] (7.6 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), and v is the scan rate (V s−1). Fig. S6 (Supplementary data) shows the CV curves of different nanomaterials at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 in the presence of 0.1 M PBS solution containing 0.1 M KCl and 0.01M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4−. The active surface area of Es is calculated to be 0.069 cm2. After coating with S-GQDs, AuNS, and AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites onto the electrode surface, the active surface areas of Es||S-GQD Es||AuNS, and Es||AuNS/S-GQDs increase to 0.17, 0.23, and 0.29 cm2, respectively, clearly indicating that the electrochemically active surface of electrode increases by a factor of 4.2 after the coating with AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites.

Fig. 3
a illustrates the LSV of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor at various scan rates of 5 – 200 mV s−1 in the potential window of -0.3 – 0.8 V. The peak oxidation current at 0.22 – 0.25 V increases as the scan rate increases from 5 to 200 mV s−1, clearly indicating that the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor developed in this study follows a diffusion-controlled electron transfer process [46]. Fig. 3
b shows the LSV curves of the stepwise modification processes of Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab electrode. The current intensity of the AuNS/S-GQDs nanocomposites can be up to 3.7 mA after coating onto the Es surface, corroborating the excellent conductivity of the AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites and their applicability to electrochemical biosensors. The current response decreases to 3.5 mA after the addition of PL to the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor. Moreover, the current intensity decreases again to 3.15 mA when the anti-PSA antibody (Ab) is attached on the Es||AuNS/S-GQD based immunosensor, presumably attributed to the fact that the antibody is an electro-inactive species which can obstruct the electron transfer process. The introduction of small BSA biomolecules, serving as the block reagent to minimize the non-specific adsorption of the AuNS/S-GQD sensing materials, leads to the decrease in current intensity of Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab to 3.07 mA. Finally, the current intensity decreases obviously to 1.1 mA when PSA is added to the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor, which elaborates that the PSA molecules can effectively link with Ab via covalent bonding, and results in the decrease in current density of immunosensor.

The EIS was then used to examine the charge transfer resistance (Rct) resistance of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD based immunosensor. Fig. 3
c displays the Nyquist plot of Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor in the frequency range of 0.1 – 10000 Hz. The Rct value of bare Es, which represents the electron transfer resistance between electrolyte and electrode surface, is observed to be 1870 Ω. After coating with S-GQDs onto the Es surface (Es||S-GQDs), the Rct value decreases significantly to 95 Ω, which signifies the excellently conductive property of S-GQDs. Interestingly, the modification of Es with AuNS/S-GQDs composites again decreases the Rct value to 11 Ω, presumably because of the addition of conductive metallic AuNPs to the active materials.

Since AuNS/S-GQDs are superior conductive materials with low impedance, the Es|| AuNS/S-GQDs is a suitable probe for PSA detection. Fig. 3
d shows the change in impedance during the step-by-step preparation of Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor in the presence of 0.1 M PBS and 0.01 M ferrocyanide at pH 6.5. After the addition of PL as the cross-linker to bind anti-PSA antibody onto the Es||AuNS/S-GQDs surface, the Rct value increases evidently to 45 Ω, elaborating the successful attachment of carboxyl-amine groups on the electrode surface. Moreover, the impedance increases to 66 Ω when Ab is added. In addition, after the BSA reagent is presented as a block agent the impedance increase to 101 Ω. The linkage of PSA with anti-PSA antibody significantly increases the impedance of electrode and the Rct increases dramatically to 210 Ω, clearly indicating that the developed Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab can effectively detect PSA.

After the successful fabrication of Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor under the optimization condition (Fig. S7, Supplementary data), the electrochemical performance of the electrode on PSA detection. In addition, the sensitivity of Es||Ab, Es||S-GQDs/Ab, Es||AuNS/Ab, and Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab toward PSA detection in the absence and presence of 50 ng mL−1 PSA was first investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S8 (Supplementary data), only 6.9% decrease in LSV current intensity of Es||Ab/BSA electrode is observed when 50 ng mL−1 PSA is addition. However, the performance of Es||S-GQDs/Ab, Es||AuNS/Ab, and Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab toward PSA enhance the change in LSV current intensity of Es||S-GQDs/Ab, Es||AuNS/Ab, and Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab toward PSA detection enhance 27%, 62%, and 90%, respectively, corroborating the fact that the AuNS/S-GQDs is conducive to enhance the biosensing performance of PSA detection. The well-defined 3D architecture of the AuNS serves as the support for the homogeneous deposition of S-GQDs as well as for the improvement of conductivity for electron transfer, while S-GQDs enhance the surface binding of Ab via the amide bonding between the amine groups of Ab and the carboxyl groups of S-GQDs [13], resulting in the superior electrochemical sensing ability toward PSA detection.

The oxidation peak currents from LSV measurement at various concentrations of PSA are displayed in Fig. 4
a. The current intensity decreases upon the increase in PSA concentrations from 0 to 50 ng mL−1. An inversely proportional relationship between LSV current intensity and PSA concentration is shown in Fig. 4
b. It is clear that a two-phase linear relationship is obtained, and the relationship between current intensity and PSA concentrations follows the linear equations of Y = -0.0098 X + 0.003 and Y = -0.22X + 1.54 with excellent correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.997 and 0.994, respectively, when PSA concentrations are in the range of 0.01 – 150 pg mL−1 and 0.15 – 50 ng mL−1, respectively ( Figs 4
c,d).

The sensitivity of the immunosensor was evaluated in 10 times diluted human serum. The relationship between current intensity and PSA concentration followed the linear equations of Y = -0.01 X + 0.003 and Y = -2.2 × 10−5 X + 0.001 with good correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.993 and 0.975, respectively, when PSA concentrations were in the range of 0.03 – 150 pg mL−1 and 0.15 – 50 ng mL−1, respectively (Fig. S9, Supplementary data). In addition, the analytical performance of Es||AuNS/S-GQDs immunosensor toward PSA detection is also evaluated in neat human serum. As shown in Fig. S10 (Supplementary data), the LSV current intensity decreases upon the increase in PSA concentration in neat human serum. A good linear PSA detection range of 0.1 – 200 pg mL−1 (Y = -0.00066 X + 0.00295, R2 = 0989) and 0.2 – 35 ng mL−1 (Y = -4.42 × 10−5 X + 0.00164, R2 = 0.983) in neat human serum is clearly obtained ( Fig. 4
,f). The excellent analytical performance of Es||AuNS/S-GQDs immunosensor in both neat and diluted human serums signifies the superior applicability of the developed immunosensor in real sample.

Several studies have used GQD based nanomaterials as the sensing elements and found that a two-phase linearity was established to detect a wide variety of analytes [36,47], which is quite similar to the results in this study. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) of Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor, determined as the 3 times of the relative standard deviation after seven measurements of the lowest concentration of PSA are calculated to be 0.29 (9.7 aM), 1.1, and 26 fg mL−1 in PBS, 10 × diluted, and neat human serums, respectively. The detection of PSA at such a low concentration is important for the early prognosis as well as the diagnosis of prostate-related diseases, and, therefore, the results observed in this study strongly highlight the promising of the developed Es||AuNS/S-GQD based immunosensor for cancer marker detection.

Several sensing technologies including optical and electrochemical method have been used to detect PSA in different matrices [8,48]. Table 1
summaries the analytical performance of electrochemical and optical nanosensors for PSA detection. For electrochemical immunosensor, several nanomaterials including PdAuPt/COOH-rGO, PtCu-hollow nanoframes, and GO/AgNPs have been used as the electrochemical immunosensing probe for PSA detection [12,13,15]. The linear range of 2 – 4 orders of magnitude with LOD of 0.33 – 270 pg mL−1 is obtained [12,48]. In this study, the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor exhibits the superior LOD value of 0.29 fg mL−1, which is 3 orders of magnitude lower than those obtained from the previously reported materials. Moreover, the wide dynamic range of 10 fg mL−1 – 50 ng mL−1 and 30 fg mL−1 – 50 ng mL−1 (6 orders of magnitude) in PBS and human serum are also superior to those published results ( Table 1 ). The wide detection linear range with attomolar LOD of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor is mainly attributed to excellent electron transfer ability and high electric conductivity of the AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites, resulting in the ultra-sensitive analytical performance on PSA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanosensing of PSA in human serum
ce:section.ce:para:
The applicability of Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor was further investigated by using human serum sample as the matrix. In this study, human serum sample was diluted 10 times with PBS and then various PSA concentrations ranging between 0.001 and 50 ng mL−1 were added into the human serum samples. Table 2
compares the recovery of PSA in human serum sample using the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor. The recovery of PSA at low concentrations of 1 – 50 pg mL−1 is in the range of 97 – 107 % and still can maintain the recovery at 105% and 98 – 100% when the PSA concentration increases to medium (0.1 – 1 ng mL−1) and high (5 – 50 ng mL−1) concentration, respectively. Moreover, the relative standard deviation is in the range of 1.7 – 4.9 % (n = 5), elaborating that the Es||AuNS/S-GQD based immunosensor not only exhibits the superior analytical performance but also shows the excellent reliability in biosensing of PSA in biological matrix environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and selectivity of AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The pH plays one of the crucial roles in influencing the stability of sensing performance and, consequently, the influence of pH on the electrochemical by sensing performance of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor is analyzed as well. Fig. 5
a displays the influence of buffer pH on the change in current signal of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor in the absence and presence of 20 ng mL−1 PSA at pHs 5 – 8. In the absence of PSA, the peak intensity increases upon the increasing pH from 5 to 6.5, and then reaches a plateau at pH 6.5 – 7.0. The further increase in pH slightly decreases the current intensity of the immunosensor. In contrast, the current intensity of immunosensor decreases obviously when 20 ng mL−1 of PSA is added, showing that the developed Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor can effectively detect PSA at all the pH values examined in this study. The current intensity decreases slightly from 17.2 ± 0.7% at pH 5 to 13.5 ± 0.4% at pH 6.5, and the increase a little bit to 15.1 ± 0.4% at pH 8. These results clearly indicate that pH 6.5 is optimal for the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor to detect PSA.

The long-term stability of immunosensor is further assessed by evaluating the analytical performance of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor toward PSA detection. Several Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor were stored at 4 °C for 6 weeks and the sample containing 50 ng mL−1 of PSA was analyzed once a week using the stored Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor. As displayed in Fig. 5
b, the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor can retain > 99% of activity during the first three weeks of storage. The activity decreases slightly after 3 weeks, and 87.2% of its original activity toward PSA detection still can be maintained at the end of sixth week, highlighting the excellent long term stability of AuNS/S-GQDs based immunosensor.

The selectivity of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor was further investigated by adding 8 different biomolecules into 10 × diluted human serum. In Fig. 5
c, the LSV current intensity ratio of immunosensor toward 8 interfering compounds including other cancer markers (α-fetoprotein, tau protein, and CEA), protein (BSA, hemoglobin), amino acid (cysteine and glutathione), and ion (choline) is in the range of 85 – 98% in comparison with the blank control. However, the current intensity decreases dramatically and only 5.1 ± 0.2% of the current intensity remains when 30 ng mL−1 PSA is added, signifying the superior selectivity toward PSA detection. Furthermore, the selectivity of the Es||AuNS/S-GQD/Ab immunosensor is evaluated in a mixture containing PSA and all the other interfering molecules. A 95.1 ± 0.1% decrease in LSV current intensity in the mixture is observed. The remaining current intensity of 4.9% is almost identical to that of pure PSA sample, clearly showing that the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor is extremely sensitive and selective toward PSA detection in a mixture with different interfering molecules.

The cytotoxicity of Es||AuNS/S-GQD was further evaluated by using TRAMP-C1, a cellosaurus cell line derived from a mouse with prostate-tumor, as the model cells. TRAMP-C1 was originally incubated with different concentrations of AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites, and the cell viability was calculated by a microplate reader. As shown in Fig. 5
d, the viability of TRAMP-C1 cells at of 100 – 1000 μg mL−1 AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites ranges from 88 to 105%, which deciphers the low cytotoxic effect of AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposites on TRAMP-C1 cells when compared to the blank sample. These results clearly indicate that AuNS/S-GQD nanocomposite exhibits a superior electrochemical performance with low cytotoxicity effect on tumor cells, which is a potential material for electrochemical biosensing of PSA.

In this study, we have successfully synthesized a novel and ultrasensitive electrochemical Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor for highly sensitive detection of PSA in both PBS and human serum. The 3-D AuNS/S-GQDs are facile fabricated at room temperature with a star-shaped configuration in the diameter range of 35 – 60 nm and a mean particle size of 50 ± 5 nm. The well-defined 3D AuNS serves as the support for the homogeneous deposition of S-GQDs as well as for the improvement of conductivity for electron transfer, while 2 – 8 nm S-GQDs enhance the binding affinity of Ab through an amide bonding between the –NH2 of Ab and the –COOH of S-GQDs, resulting in the superior electrochemical sensing ability toward PSA detection. The Es||AuNS/S-GQDs immunosensor exhibits good biocompatibility, enhanced current response, high stability, and extraordinary electrocatalytic performance towards PSA detection, and the developed immunosensor displays a wide linear range of 5 – 6 orders of magnitude from 10 fg mL−1 to 50 ng mL−1, from 30 fg mL−1 to 50 ng mL−1, and from 100 fg mL−1 to 35 ng mL−1 with extremely low LOD of 0.29 (9.7 aM), 1.1 fg mL−1, and 0.026 pg mL−1 in PBS, 10 × diluted, and neat human serums, respectively. A highly selective PSA detection over 8 different interfering biomarkers and proteins is also observed. Moreover, the Es||AuNS/S-GQD immunosensor exhibits excellent biosensing performance toward PSA detection in human serum with recovery in the range of 97 – 107%. The AuNS/S-GQDs shows a low cytotoxic effect on TRAMP-C1. These results demonstrate the superiority of the Es||AuNS/S-GQDs immunosensor for ultra-highly sensitive and selective biosensing of attomolar level of PSA and other cancer markers for early clinical diagnosis.
